name: AP Computer Science A
description: AP Computer Science A exam preparation following the College Board Course and Exam Description. Students learn Java programming, object-oriented design, arrays and ArrayLists, 2D arrays, inheritance, polymorphism, recursion, and algorithmic problem solving while preparing for the College Board AP exam and introductory college CS expectations.

modules.title: Primitive Types & Variables
modules.objectives: Write Java statements that declare, initialize, and update int, double, boolean, and char variables; trace arithmetic expressions with operator precedence; explain integer division vs. floating-point division; apply numeric casts safely; format console output with System.out.println.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Bro Code — Java Tutorial for Beginners (variables and operators)
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write a Java program that converts a temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit and from Fahrenheit to Celsius. The program should accept a number and a unit ('C' or 'F') from the user and print the conversion. Demonstrate that you understand integer vs. double division by testing 5/2 and 5.0/2 and explaining the difference in a code comment. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Primitive Types & Variables module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes
modules.titleZh: 使用对象：String、Math 与包装类
modules.objectives: Distinguish primitive types from reference types; create String objects and call methods (length, substring, indexOf, equals, compareTo); use Math methods (sqrt, pow, abs, random); understand Integer and Double wrapper classes; recognize that Strings are immutable. Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: CodingBat — String-1 (15 problems) — for independent practice on Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes before submitting the assignment
modules.assignment: Write a program that takes a person's full name as a single String ('First Last' format) and prints: their initials, the length of the last name only, the name in uppercase, and whether the first name comes before the last name alphabetically. Use only String methods — no array splitting yet. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Booleans & if Statements
modules.titleZh: 布尔值与 if 语句
modules.objectives: Write boolean expressions using comparison and logical operators (&&, ||, !); apply short-circuit evaluation; structure code with if, if-else, and else-if chains; understand the equals method vs. == for Strings.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write a program that classifies a triangle given three side lengths. Output 'equilateral', 'isosceles', 'scalene', or 'not a triangle' based on the rules (each side less than the sum of the other two). Include three test cases that exercise each branch and one that tests the 'not a triangle' case. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Booleans & if Statements module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Iteration: while and for Loops
modules.titleZh: 迭代：while 与 for 循环
modules.objectives: Write while loops with correct termination conditions; convert between while and for loops; write loops that count up, count down, and step by k; identify and avoid infinite loops; trace loop variables by hand.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write three loop programs: (1) print the multiplication table from 1 to 10, (2) compute the sum of all even numbers from 2 to N where N is given, (3) print the digits of an integer in reverse order using a while loop with division and modulus. Include trace tables for the first two iterations of each. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Iteration: while and for Loops module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Nested Loops & Loop Patterns
modules.objectives: Write nested loops to traverse two-dimensional patterns; recognize the standard patterns: counter, accumulator, max/min, sentinel-controlled; understand loop complexity (single loop vs. nested) at an intuitive level. Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write three nested-loop programs: (1) print a right-angled triangle of asterisks of height N, (2) print a multiplication table grid 1..10 × 1..10 with neat column alignment, (3) given a 2D pattern of letters as a String[][], find the most common letter. Add a comment estimating how many comparisons each program does for input N or grid size G. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Nested Loops & Loop Patterns module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Writing Classes & Methods
modules.objectives: Define a class with instance variables and methods; pass parameters and return values correctly; understand method signatures and overloading; document methods with concise comments and parameter descriptions. Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing lesson resource for this topic: Writing Classes & Methods.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing and grammar exercises: Writing Classes & Methods.
modules.assignment: Design a Rectangle class with width and height instance variables, a constructor, and methods getArea(), getPerimeter(), isSquare(), and scale(double factor). Write a separate driver class that creates three Rectangles and prints each one's area, perimeter, and whether it is a square. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Writing Classes & Methods module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Constructors, Encapsulation & this
modules.titleZh: 构造函数、封装与 this 关键字
modules.objectives: Write constructors that initialize instance variables; understand the role of 'this' in disambiguating parameters from fields; apply encapsulation by making instance variables private and exposing only getters/setters; explain why encapsulation matters for invariants.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Refactor your Rectangle class from Module 6: make all instance variables private, add a no-arg constructor (default 1×1) and a two-arg constructor, validate inputs (no negative dimensions — throw IllegalArgumentException), and add a setWidth/setHeight pair that re-validates. Write tests that demonstrate the validation works. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Constructors, Encapsulation & this module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Static vs. Instance
modules.objectives: Explain the difference between static class-level members and instance object-level members; apply static for constants, counters, and utility methods; access static members with ClassName.member; trace why main is static and why static methods cannot directly use instance fields.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Add a static class variable 'rectanglesCreated' to your Rectangle class that tracks how many Rectangle objects have been instantiated. Add a static method getRectangleCount(). Demonstrate from a driver: create 5 Rectangles, then print getRectangleCount() — it should be 5. Explain in a comment why this variable cannot be an instance variable. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Static vs. Instance module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Arrays (1D)
modules.objectives: Write and trace Java code that declares, initializes, indexes, and updates 1D arrays; iterate with for and enhanced for-each loops; identify ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException causes; implement helper methods that receive arrays and demonstrate reference semantics.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Bro Code — 1D Arrays in Java — for a visual explanation of Arrays (1D) before guided practice
modules.video2Note: CodingBat — Array-1 (15 problems) — for a visual explanation of Arrays (1D) before guided practice
modules.assignment: Write four methods that operate on an int[] array: (1) sum, (2) max, (3) reverse (in place), (4) countAbove(int threshold). Write a driver that creates an array, prints it, calls each method, and prints results. Explain in a comment why reverse modifies the original array but sum returns a new value. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Arrays (1D) module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: ArrayList
modules.objectives: Implement Java programs that import and use ArrayList<E>; apply add, remove, get, set, and size correctly; trace index shifting after insertions and removals; compare ArrayList with primitive arrays; convert between ArrayList and array when an API requires it.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write a TodoList class backed by an ArrayList<String>. Methods: add(String task), removeAt(int index), markDone(int index) (which prepends '[X] '), printAll(). Write a driver that adds 5 tasks, marks 2 done, removes 1, and prints the list. Then convert the final list to a String[] and print its length. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the ArrayList module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: 2D Arrays
modules.objectives: Declare and initialize 2D arrays with [rows][cols]; iterate with nested loops in row-major order; understand 2D arrays as arrays of arrays (jagged arrays possible); apply to grid problems (game boards, image pixels, matrices). Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Implement a Tic-Tac-Toe board as char[3][3]. Write methods: printBoard(), placeMove(int row, int col, char player), isWinner(char player), and isFull(). Demonstrate with a driver that simulates a complete game (alternating X and O) until someone wins or the board is full. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the 2D Arrays module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Inheritance & Polymorphism
modules.objectives: Define subclasses with extends; call super() in constructors; override methods (with @Override); use polymorphism (a parent reference holding a child object); understand the relationship between Object class and all other classes; cast safely with instanceof. Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Build a small class hierarchy: Animal (parent) → Dog and Cat (children). Animal has name, age, and a speak() method that prints 'Some sound'. Dog overrides speak() to print 'Woof'; Cat to print 'Meow'. Write a driver that creates an Animal[] containing a Dog and a Cat, and loops through calling speak() — demonstrate polymorphism in action. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Inheritance & Polymorphism module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Recursion
modules.objectives: Identify a base case and a recursive case; trace recursive calls with a call stack diagram; convert simple iterative algorithms to recursive ones; recognize when recursion is natural (tree traversal, divide-and-conquer) vs. forced.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Bro Code — Recursion Visualized — for a visual explanation of Recursion before guided practice
modules.assignment: Implement these methods recursively: factorial(int n), fibonacci(int n), sumDigits(int n), and palindromeCheck(String s). For at least one method, draw a call stack diagram showing every recursive call for an input of 4. Compare runtime intuitively (which one would be slow on n=40 and why). Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Recursion module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Sorting & Searching Algorithms
modules.objectives: Implement and trace selection sort, insertion sort, and merge sort; implement linear search and binary search; understand the requirement that binary search needs sorted input; recognize O(n²) vs. O(n log n) vs. O(log n) at an intuitive level. Students will use vocabulary, models, and evidence to interpret examples, support scientific explanations, and connect the concept to the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Bro Code — Selection, Insertion, and Merge Sort
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Implement selectionSort, insertionSort, and binarySearch on int[] arrays. Write a driver that builds an array of 20 random ints, sorts it with each algorithm (verify all three give the same sorted result), then runs binarySearch on the sorted output for 5 different target values (3 in the array, 2 not in the array). Print the index returned (or -1). Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Sorting & Searching Algorithms module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: What is the value of result after this code runs? int result = 7 / 2;
quizQuestions.explanation: Integer division drops the decimal part, so 7 / 2 evaluates to 3. Students need to recognize why 3 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which declaration stores a decimal value most appropriately in Java?
quizQuestions.explanation: double stores floating-point decimal values. This reinforces the Primitive Types & Variables idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: What does casting do in this expression: (double) 5 / 2?
options: Changes 5 to a floating-point value before division
Rounds the answer to the nearest integer
quizQuestions.answer: Changes 5 to a floating-point value before division
quizQuestions.explanation: Casting 5 to double makes the division floating-point, producing 2.5. Students need to recognize why Changes 5 to a floating-point value before division matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which String comparison is safest for checking same text content?
quizQuestions.explanation: equals compares String contents; == compares object references. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes.

quizQuestions.question: What does "computer".substring(0, 3) return?
quizQuestions.explanation: substring starts at index 0 and stops before index 3. This reinforces the Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: What is true about String objects in Java?
options: They can only store one character
quizQuestions.explanation: String methods return new values; they do not change the original String. It connects the correct answer to Using Objects: String, Math, and Wrapper Classes and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Which expression is true when x is between 10 and 20 inclusive?
quizQuestions.explanation: Java requires two comparisons joined by && for an inclusive range. This reinforces the Booleans & if Statements idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Why can short-circuit evaluation matter?
options: It can skip evaluating the right side of && or ||
quizQuestions.answer: It can skip evaluating the right side of && or ||
quizQuestions.explanation: For &&, Java skips the right side if the left side is false; for ||, it skips if the left side is true.

quizQuestions.question: Which operator means logical NOT in Java?
quizQuestions.explanation: ! reverses a boolean value. != means not equal. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Booleans & if Statements.

quizQuestions.question: How many times does this loop run? for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
quizQuestions.explanation: i takes values 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. This reinforces the Iteration: while and for Loops idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which loop is best when you do not know in advance how many repetitions are needed?
quizQuestions.explanation: A while loop continues until its condition becomes false, useful for sentinel input or unknown repetition.

quizQuestions.question: What is a common cause of an infinite loop?
options: The loop variable is never updated toward stopping
quizQuestions.answer: The loop variable is never updated toward stopping
quizQuestions.explanation: If the condition never changes toward false, the loop can run forever. This reinforces the Iteration: while and for Loops idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: A nested loop with outer loop n times and inner loop n times usually performs about how many iterations?
quizQuestions.explanation: The inner loop runs n times for each of n outer iterations. Students need to recognize why n squared matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which pattern is most natural for printing a grid of rows and columns?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rows and columns are a two-dimensional pattern, so nested loops fit naturally.

quizQuestions.question: What is an accumulator variable used for?
options: Storing a running total or combined result
quizQuestions.answer: Storing a running total or combined result
quizQuestions.explanation: An accumulator is updated repeatedly inside a loop. This reinforces the Nested Loops & Loop Patterns idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which part of a method signature identifies the data a method receives?
quizQuestions.explanation: Parameters define inputs passed into a method. This reinforces the Writing Classes & Methods idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: What does a return type of void mean?
quizQuestions.explanation: void methods perform actions but do not return a value. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Writing Classes & Methods.

quizQuestions.question: Why split code into methods?
options: To make code impossible to test
To organize behavior into reusable named units
quizQuestions.answer: To organize behavior into reusable named units
quizQuestions.explanation: Methods improve readability, reuse, testing, and decomposition. This reinforces the Writing Classes & Methods idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Why are instance variables usually private?
options: To support encapsulation and protect object state
Because public variables cannot store data
quizQuestions.answer: To support encapsulation and protect object state
quizQuestions.explanation: Private fields prevent outside code from changing state without validation.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary role of a constructor?
quizQuestions.explanation: A constructor sets up instance variables when an object is created. It connects the correct answer to Constructors, Encapsulation & this and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: When is the keyword this most useful?
options: When a parameter name matches an instance variable name
quizQuestions.answer: When a parameter name matches an instance variable name
quizQuestions.explanation: this.field refers to the current object field, avoiding ambiguity. This reinforces the Constructors, Encapsulation & this idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: A static variable belongs to:
quizQuestions.explanation: static members are shared at the class level. It connects the correct answer to Static vs. Instance and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Which is a good use of static?
options: A rectangle width unique to one rectangle
A student age unique to one student
quizQuestions.explanation: Class-level constants or utility methods are common static uses. It connects the correct answer to Static vs. Instance and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Why is main declared static?
options: It must run before any object of the class is created
It can only be used in abstract classes
quizQuestions.answer: It must run before any object of the class is created
quizQuestions.explanation: The JVM can call main without first constructing an object. Students need to recognize why It must run before any object of the class is created matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What exception occurs when accessing arr[arr.length]?
quizQuestions.explanation: The last valid index is arr.length - 1. Students need to recognize why ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What is true when an array is passed to a method?
options: A reference to the same array is passed
quizQuestions.answer: A reference to the same array is passed
quizQuestions.explanation: Methods can modify the array elements through the shared reference. This reinforces the Arrays (1D) idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which loop is best for visiting every value when indexes are not needed?
quizQuestions.explanation: Enhanced for loops simplify reading each element without index logic. Students need to recognize why enhanced for loop matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which statement creates an ArrayList of Strings?
quizQuestions.explanation: ArrayList uses object types as generic parameters. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from ArrayList.

quizQuestions.question: Which ArrayList method returns the element at index i?
quizQuestions.explanation: get retrieves an element by index. It connects the correct answer to ArrayList and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: How is ArrayList different from a normal array?
options: It can resize as elements are added or removed
It stores only primitive int values
quizQuestions.answer: It can resize as elements are added or removed
quizQuestions.explanation: ArrayList is a resizable list backed by an array-like structure. This reinforces the ArrayList idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: In int[][] grid = new int[3][4], how many rows are there?
quizQuestions.explanation: The first dimension is the number of rows. This reinforces the 2D Arrays idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: What traversal visits each row completely before moving to the next row?
quizQuestions.explanation: Row-major traversal loops rows outside and columns inside. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from 2D Arrays.

quizQuestions.question: Why are nested loops common with 2D arrays?
options: One loop controls rows and one controls columns
quizQuestions.answer: One loop controls rows and one controls columns
quizQuestions.explanation: A 2D grid has two dimensions, so two loop variables are usually needed. Students need to recognize why One loop controls rows and one controls columns matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which keyword creates an inheritance relationship?
quizQuestions.explanation: A subclass extends a superclass. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Inheritance & Polymorphism.

quizQuestions.question: What does method overriding allow?
options: A subclass provides its own version of an inherited method
A method runs without a name
quizQuestions.answer: A subclass provides its own version of an inherited method
quizQuestions.explanation: Overriding supports polymorphic behavior. Students need to recognize why A subclass provides its own version of an inherited method matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What does polymorphism allow?
options: A superclass reference can refer to a subclass object
A class can have no constructor
quizQuestions.answer: A superclass reference can refer to a subclass object
quizQuestions.explanation: Polymorphism lets code use a general type while executing subclass-specific methods.

quizQuestions.question: What must every recursive method have to stop correctly?
quizQuestions.explanation: The base case ends the recursion. It connects the correct answer to Recursion and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: What data structure concept is used to track recursive calls?
quizQuestions.explanation: Each recursive call gets its own stack frame. It connects the correct answer to Recursion and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Which problem is naturally recursive?
quizQuestions.explanation: Trees are recursive structures: each subtree can be traversed the same way.

quizQuestions.question: What condition is required for binary search?
options: The values must all be Strings
quizQuestions.explanation: Binary search discards halves based on sorted order. This reinforces the Sorting & Searching Algorithms idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which sorting algorithm divides the array and merges sorted halves?
quizQuestions.explanation: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm. Students need to recognize why Merge sort matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What is the average time complexity of binary search?
quizQuestions.explanation: Each comparison cuts the remaining search space roughly in half. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Sorting & Searching Algorithms.

questions.question: What is printed by System.out.println(9 / 4);?
questions.explanation: Both operands are ints, so integer division truncates. It connects the correct answer to AP Computer Science A midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Which expression correctly tests whether s equals "yes"?
questions.explanation: equals compares String contents. It connects the correct answer to AP Computer Science A midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: What is the value of !(true && false)?
questions.explanation: true && false is false; !false is true. Students need to recognize why true matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: A loop that reads values until the user types -1 is best described as:
questions.explanation: A sentinel value signals when input should stop. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from AP Computer Science A midterm concepts.

questions.question: A double nested loop over an n by n grid has which order of growth?
questions.explanation: There are n rows times n columns. It connects the correct answer to AP Computer Science A midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Which method header returns an int and receives two int parameters?
options: public int add(int a, int b)
public void add(int a, int b)
questions.answer: public int add(int a, int b)

questions.question: Why should a class validate constructor parameters?

questions.question: What is the result of "desk".indexOf("s")?

questions.question: Which algorithm is usually O(n log n)?
